task_description: Decide which caching strategy to adopt for the API layer (team: planner, researcher, critic, moderator).

conversation.speaker: moderator
conversation.text: Team, we need a caching decision for the API layer by end of session. Planner, kick us off.

conversation.speaker: planner
conversation.text: I propose we adopt a write-through Redis cache with a 5 minute TTL on read-heavy endpoints. It's simple to reason about and covers 80% of our read load.

conversation.speaker: researcher
conversation.text: Data point: our access logs show the /search endpoint has a 92% repeat-query rate within 3 minutes, but /profile reads are almost never repeated. A blanket TTL would waste memory on /profile.

conversation.speaker: critic
conversation.text: I disagree with write-through here. Our write volume spikes 40x during ingestion windows; write-through would couple ingestion latency to Redis availability. Write-behind or cache-aside degrades more gracefully.

conversation.speaker: planner
conversation.text: Fair on both counts. Revised proposal: cache-aside with per-endpoint TTLs — 5 minutes on /search, no caching on /profile, and ingestion writes bypass the cache entirely.

conversation.speaker: researcher
conversation.text: The revised numbers work: cache-aside with a 5 minute /search TTL yields an estimated 71% hit rate at one-eighth the memory of the blanket approach. I support the revision.

conversation.speaker: critic
conversation.text: I can accept cache-aside. I still want a circuit breaker so Redis outages fall back to direct reads — with that condition, I'm on board.

conversation.speaker: moderator
conversation.text: Decision recorded: cache-aside with per-endpoint TTLs and a Redis circuit breaker. Planner owns the implementation ticket.

task_description: Debate: should the product ship with opt-out telemetry? (pro, con, judge)

conversation.speaker: judge
conversation.text: Question before us: ship with opt-out telemetry enabled by default. Pro, opening statement.

conversation.speaker: pro
conversation.text: Opt-out telemetry is the only way to get representative data. Opt-in samples skew toward power users — the 3% who enable it are exactly the users whose crashes we already hear about. Default-on with a clear first-run banner is both effective and honest.

conversation.speaker: con
conversation.text: Representative data doesn't justify non-consensual collection. GDPR aside, defaults are sticky precisely because most users never see settings — calling that 'consent' is a fiction. And one telemetry incident costs more trust than all the crash data is worth.

conversation.speaker: pro
conversation.text: The trust argument cuts both ways: users also lose trust when bugs persist for months because we're debugging blind. On GDPR — crash traces with no user content qualify as legitimate interest under Recital 47; this is settled practice for major OSes.

conversation.speaker: con
conversation.text: I'll concede that anonymized crash-only telemetry is a different category from behavioral analytics — if the proposal were scoped to crash reports only, my objection narrows considerably.

conversation.speaker: pro
conversation.text: I accept that scoping: crash and hang reports only, no feature usage tracking, payloads published in the docs. That is our amended position.

conversation.speaker: judge
conversation.text: Ruling: opt-out telemetry limited to anonymized crash/hang reports with documented payloads; behavioral analytics remain opt-in. Pro prevails on the narrowed question; Con's concession materially improved the outcome.

task_description: Groupthink case: choose a database for the new analytics service (lead, dev_a, dev_b).

conversation.speaker: lead
conversation.text: I think we should just use Mongo for the analytics service, same as everything else. Objections?

conversation.speaker: dev_a
conversation.text: Sounds good to me. Consistency with the other services is worth a lot.

conversation.speaker: dev_b
conversation.text: Well — analytics is mostly aggregation over columns, and our queries are... actually, never mind, Mongo is fine.

conversation.speaker: lead
conversation.text: Great, unanimous. Mongo it is — I'll set up the cluster today.

conversation.speaker: dev_a
conversation.text: Agreed, let's move fast.
